notes.note: Cicero's son Marcus, then studying (or about to study) at Athens; Atticus had written the young man a letter of admonition that Cicero here praises for its firm but measured tone.
The Tullii are Cicero's own freedmen and dependents bearing his gentile name, who looked after the young Marcus's affairs; the surface could not be bound to a single registered entity.

notes.anchor: I shall have the gardens from your hand
notes.note: The "gardens" (horti) are a suburban pleasure-estate Cicero is trying to buy through Atticus; the purchase is meant to house a shrine (fanum) to his dead daughter Tullia, the great project running through these months.
Cicero had drafted a letter of political advice to Caesar and was anxiously awaiting the verdict of Caesar's confidants (Balbus and Oppius, "those people") before sending it.
Sextus Peducaeus the younger, a close friend of both Cicero and Atticus; Cicero says he now loves the son as much as he had valued the father of the same name.
